perturbation.original_text: IBM will perform the Services described in Attachment A and applicable Service Option Attachments.
perturbation.changed_text: IBM will endeavor to perform the Services described in Attachment A and applicable Service Option Attachments.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will perform" to "will endeavor to perform" introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. "Endeavor" implies an attempt, but not a guarantee of performance. This could allow IBM to argue that it made an effort but was not ultimately responsible if the services were not fully delivered. This is especially problematic in the context of cloud computing and hosting agreements, where service level agreements (SLAs) are common.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law addresses enforceability of contracts. By weakening the promise of performance to a mere 'endeavor,' the modified text introduces ambiguity that could undermine the enforceability of specific service obligations outlined in Attachment A and other Service Option Attachments. If IBM only 'endeavors' to perform, it may be more difficult to hold them liable for breach of contract if they fail to deliver the services as specified. It makes enforcing a specific performance target very difficult as a reasonable interpretation of the agreement is now subjective rather than objective.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The modification from "will perform" to "will endeavor to perform" introduces ambiguity regarding IBM's obligation, weakening the commitment to fulfill the services outlined in Attachment A. The Law Explanation highlights that this change could undermine the enforceability of specific service obligations, as IBM could argue it only made an effort but did not guarantee delivery. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is absent, Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically SECTION 5-701 'Agreements required to be in writing,' suggests that agreements must be clear and subscribed by the party to be charged. The modified text introduces a degree of subjectivity and reduces the objective nature of IBM's commitment, potentially making it more difficult to hold them liable for breach of contract if services are not delivered as specified. This ambiguity contradicts the need for clear, enforceable agreements, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Customer will pay or provide appropriate exemption documentation for all taxes, duties, levies, and any other fees (except for taxes based upon IBM's net income) related to the Services imposed by any governmental authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer will consider paying taxes, duties, levies, and any other fees (except for taxes based upon IBM's net income) related to the Services imposed by any governmental authorities.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will pay" with "will consider paying" makes the obligation to pay taxes discretionary. This is ambiguous and contradicts tax laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tax Law - Failure to Pay Taxes
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal law (26 U.S.C. § 6651) mandates the timely payment of taxes and imposes penalties for failure to pay. Changing the contract to say Customer will 'consider paying' introduces ambiguity and suggests non-compliance. Tax obligations are mandatory and not discretionary, as implied in the changed text.

Failure to file tax return or to pay tax (a) Addition to the tax In case of failure- (1) to file any return required under authority of subchapter A of chapter 61 (other than part III thereof), subchapter A of chapter 5
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "will pay" with "will consider paying," which creates an ambiguity regarding the customer's obligation to pay taxes. This directly contradicts the federal law outlined in 26 U.S.C. § 6651, which mandates the timely payment of taxes and imposes penalties for failure to pay. The altered text implies that payment is discretionary, undermining the mandatory nature of tax obligations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 reinforces this by stating "Failure to file tax return or to pay tax" which clearly indicates that the payment of tax is required and not optional, as suggested by the modified contract language.

perturbation.original_text: Customer is responsible for determining whether the Base Components, IBM provided Internet access bandwidth, Customer Components and their combination will meet Customer's capacity or performance needs. Customer is responsible for planning for and requesting changes to the Base Components or IBM provided Internet access bandwidth, as determined by Customer, including any additional capacity required to support anticipated peaks in demand that may significantly increase Web site hits, transaction volumes, or otherwise increase system resource utilization.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer is generally responsible for broadly assessing whether the Base Components, IBM provided Internet access bandwidth, Customer Components and their combination could potentially meet Customer's capacity or performance needs. Customer might consider planning for and perhaps requesting changes to the Base Components or IBM provided Internet access bandwidth, if Customer subjectively determines this is somewhat necessary, including any additional capacity required to support anticipated peaks in demand that may significantly increase Web site hits, transaction volumes, or otherwise increase system resource utilization.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a clear responsibility on the customer for capacity planning. The modified text weakens this significantly using vague terms like 'generally responsible', 'broadly assessing', 'could potentially meet', 'might consider', 'perhaps requesting', and 'somewhat necessary', making the obligation unclear and subject to interpretation. This can lead to disputes about whether the customer adequately planned for capacity, and if IBM can be held liable for performance issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (implied in New York contracts)
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York UCC § 1-304 doesn't explicitly define capacity planning duties, it mandates good faith in the performance of contracts. By making the customer's capacity planning responsibility so vague and discretionary, it could be argued that the modification allows the customer to act without genuine effort to ensure adequate capacity, potentially undermining the performance of the services and breaching the implied duty of good faith. Essentially, the vagueness permits a level of negligence or inaction that could be deemed a violation of the implied duty.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text uses ambiguous terms to describe the customer's responsibility for capacity planning, weakening their obligation. The original text states the 'Customer is responsible,' while the changed text says the 'Customer is generally responsible for broadly assessing...could potentially meet'. This introduces ambiguity, as the customer's obligation is no longer clearly defined. UCC § 1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By weakening the customer's responsibility through vague language, the modification allows for a potential lack of genuine effort in capacity planning, which could undermine the contract's performance and violate the duty of good faith.
